Plaintiff Angelica Perez'S Motion To Quash Subpoenas
Off Calendar Aug-22-2014 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
RULING: The Court adopts its tentative ruling on Plaintiff Angelica Perez's Motion To Quash Subpoenas as follows: The motion to quash is granted as to all subpoenas. The subpoenas were served by the defendant to obtain plaintiff's personnel and medical records, generally speaking. However, this is an action for violation of the San Francisco ordinance regarding owner move-in evictions. The plaintiff has disclaimed any damages for loss of wages or personal injury, other than emotional distress damages relating to the eviction itself. The records sought by defendant are protected by a constitutional right to privacy. Defendant must come forward with a compelling reason why these documents are relevant to the case and the information cannot be obtained by less intrusive means. None of the arguments offered by Defendant are persuasive. It is not relevant whether plaintiff listed the apartment from which she was evicted as her primary residence, or listed her father as an occupant. The medical records are not sufficiently germane to plaintiff's alleged injury to overcome their protected status. Finally, Defendant identifies none of her more than twenty affirmative defenses to which the subpoenaed information would relate. Accordingly, the Court quashes the subpoenas. Plaintiff also seeks sanctions for the time spent bringing the motions to quash. Plaintiff points out that she sought to meet and confer over the subpoenas, but received no response, a fact which Defendant does not dispute. The Court finds that sanctions are warranted as to the subpoenas for personnel records, for which there is no substantial justification and as to which Defendant did not meet and confer. The subpoenas for medical records have a more arguably legitimate basis, although not one ultimately supported by the arguments Defendant has presented. Accordingly, the Court grants the motion for sanctions in the amount of $700. Plaintiff's counsel submits a proposed order.
